nuggets.headline: Radiohead scrapped the initial sessions for *In Rainbows* before reuniting with producer Nigel Godrich
nuggets.text: The journey to *In Rainbows* was fraught with difficulty. After tense sessions for their previous album, the band tried working with producer Mark "Spike" Stent, but the results felt dead. Frustrated, they scrapped the work and called their crucial collaborator, Nigel Godrich, who prescribed an "adventure": recording in a crumbling, supposedly haunted Georgian mansion to reset their chemistry and start from scratch.

source.title: The Making of Radiohead's 'In Rainbows'
source.quoteSnippet: The sessions – their first in a decade with a producer other than Godrich... were a dead end. 'Nigel thought we needed an adventure,' guitarist Ed O’Brien told Rolling Stone. The producer dragged them to Tottenham House... 'It was really old and literally crumbling,' Godrich says. 'Like a Scooby Doo mansion.'

nuggets.imageCaption: Producer Nigel Godrich, often called Radiohead's "sixth member."

nuggets.headline: the shimmering effect in "Weird Fishes/Arpeggi" comes from three interlocking, rhythmically distinct guitar parts
nuggets.text: The song's subtitle, "Arpeggi," points to its core structure. You're hearing three separate guitar arpeggios layered together, played by Ed O'Brien, Thom Yorke, and Jonny Greenwood. Each guitarist uses a different accent pattern over the eighth-note grid, creating a complex polyrhythmic texture that feels like it's phasing in and out of time—an effect often compared to the work of minimalist composer Steve Reich.

source.title: A Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead’s “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi” – Flypaper
source.quoteSnippet: The accents are here summarized (counted in eighth notes over 4-bar patterns...): Gtr III (Greenwood): 232323 2323232*, Gtr II (Yorke): 323232 323232*, Gtr I (O’Brien): 233332 233332.

nuggets.imageCaption: A transcription of Ed O'Brien's opening arpeggio for the track.

nuggets.headline: Thom Yorke and Nigel Godrich formed Atoms for Peace to perform Yorke's solo album live
nuggets.text: If the dense, rhythmic layering of this track resonates, explore Atoms for Peace. The band, which includes Thom Yorke, producer Nigel Godrich, and Flea of the Red Hot Chili Peppers, was originally formed to play the songs from Yorke's solo album *The Eraser* live. Start with their 2013 album *AMOK* to hear where these rhythmic ideas went next.

source.title: Nigel Godrich
source.quoteSnippet: Godrich is a member of the bands Atoms for Peace (with Yorke) and Ultraísta.

nuggets.imageCaption: The supergroup Atoms for Peace, featuring Thom Yorke and Nigel Godrich.

artistSummary: Radiohead are masters of texture and tension, evolving from '90s alt-rock icons to experimental electronic pioneers. Their long-standing collaboration with producer Nigel Godrich has defined their sound, pushing them to create meticulously crafted albums that reward deep listening.
